name: You get it now — where next?
tagline: A week ago these words meant nothing to you. Now you can hold your own in any AI conversation — you know what AI actually is, what an agent is, why there are so many models, what a context window and RAG and MCP are. That's not being an expert; it's being literate, and literacy is the hard part done. This last module recaps everything you now own in one line each, then points you at the right next door — no theory, no pressure, just a clear signpost.
overview: This is the send-off. It teaches no new theory — it can't, because you already have the whole vocabulary. Its one job is to make you feel how far you've come and then route you to the right next step. You arrived confused about a wall of jargon: agent, model, open vs closed, temperature, context window, memory, RAG, knowledge graph, MCP. Every one of those now has a plain meaning you could explain to a colleague, and each came with an everyday picture — AI as a brilliant, well-read new hire; context as the desk it works at; memory as the notebook beside the desk; MCP as USB-C for AI tools. Lesson one is a warm, honest recap: each of the eleven ideas from modules 00 through 11, restated in your own new words, so you can see the shape of what you've built. Lesson two is the router. It recaps all eleven ideas one more time — one line each — and then points at the two doors honestly and without selling. If you want to USE AI in your business, the next step is AI Business Foundations: plain, hands-on, and you finish by shipping one real automation. If you want to go PRO and get certified, there's a whole practitioner lane that takes you from developer to architect. And if you'd like a first recognised credential without going deep-technical, Google's Generative AI Leader is a friendly one to aim at. The honest framing throughout: completion here means literacy, not expertise. You can define the words and choose your door with confidence — and everything from here is just building on words you already own.
objectives: Recap, in your own plain words, the eleven core ideas this course gave you — what AI is, what an agent is, text vs images vs video, why there are so many models and how to choose, open vs closed, the knobs, context, memory, RAG, knowledge graphs, and MCP — and recognise that you can now define each without jargon.
Name honestly what "completion" means here: not that you're an AI expert, but that you're AI-literate — you can hold your own in the conversation and pick a sensible next step.

Choose your next door with confidence — AI Business Foundations to use AI in your business, the practitioner lane to get certified, or Google's Generative AI Leader as a gentle first credential — knowing what each one is for.

lessons.title: Look how far you've come: the whole vocabulary, in your own words
lessons.objective: Feel the distance travelled — restate each of the eleven ideas from modules 00–11 in plain, confident language, and see that together they form a mental model you actually own.
lessons.body:
## A week ago, none of this meant anything

Cast your mind back to the start of this course. "Agent." "Model." "Context window." "RAG." "MCP." A wall of letters and buzzwords, thrown around by people who never stopped to explain them, in a way that made you feel like everyone was in on a secret except you.

Look where you are now. Every one of those words has a plain, ordinary meaning you could explain to a friend over coffee. You didn't learn to code. You didn't memorise anything technical. You just got the *ideas* — one confusion killed at a time, each one wrapped in a picture from everyday life. That's worth pausing on before you take the next step, because it's a bigger thing than it feels like from the inside.

So let's walk back through what you now own. Not to teach it again — you have it — but so you can *see the shape* of it all together.

## The one idea everything hangs on

**What AI actually is.** Underneath every product and every headline, today's AI is a system that read an enormous amount of text and learned to **predict likely next words**. The picture we used was a **brilliant, well-read new hire**: fast, tireless, has a fair first answer for almost anything — and prone to confident guessing, and clueless about your specific business until you show it. That single image — a capable new hire, not an all-knowing oracle — is the spine that everything else hangs off. Manage it like a new hire and it amazes you.

## The eleven ideas, restated in your words

Here is the whole course, one idea at a time, in the plain language you now speak:

- **What an agent is.** Not just a chatbot that talks back. An agent is that well-read new hire **given an actual job** — a role, the knowledge it needs, and tools it's allowed to use — so it can go and *do* the work, not just describe it.
- **Text, images, video.** The same underlying trick — learn the patterns, predict what comes next — pointed at **different materials**. One kind of model works with words, another with pictures, another with moving footage. Same idea, different medium.
- **Why there are so many models.** GPT, Claude, Gemini, Llama and the rest aren't a confusing mob — they're **different brands of a similar tool**, each with its own strengths and personality, the way there are many good cars or many good phones.
- **How to choose a model.** You don't crown a single "best." You **pick by the job** in front of you, and — crucially — you stay **free to switch**, because tying yourself to one forever is the only real mistake.
- **Open vs closed.** The plainest version: **rent versus own.** Closed models you access as a service someone else runs; open models you can take and run yourself. Neither is "better" — they're different trade-offs.
- **The knobs.** The settings you *can* fiddle with — you mostly **leave them alone**. The one worth knowing is **temperature**: low for careful and predictable, higher for loose and creative. The rest you can happily ignore.
- **Context — the desk.** The AI's **short-term working space**, the desk it lays your conversation out on while it works. A **bigger desk holds more at once** — but the desk is **wiped clean at the end of each chat**. That's why a fresh conversation starts from nothing.
- **Memory — the notebook.** The fix for the wiped desk: a **notebook kept beside the desk** where useful things get written down, so they survive after the chat ends. Context is what's on the desk *now*; memory is what was saved for *next time*.
- **Teaching AI your stuff (RAG).** How the AI comes to know about *your* world: you **give it your documents**, and it can look things up in them when it answers. That's RAG — the difference between a generic reply and one grounded in your actual prices, policies, and facts.
- **Knowledge graphs.** Not just a pile of documents, but a **map of how your things connect** — which customer belongs to which account, which project links to which person — so the AI can follow the relationships, not just read the pages.
- **MCP.** **USB-C for AI tools.** One agreed-on plug so any AI that speaks it can connect to any tool that speaks it — your calendar, your files, your apps — without a custom cable hand-built for every single pairing.

## That's a whole mental model, not a glossary

Read that list back and notice something: it isn't eleven disconnected facts. It's a **story that fits together.** A capable new hire (what AI is), given a job (an agent), that you pick for the task and stay free to swap (models, open vs closed), tuned lightly if at all (the knobs), working at a desk that gets wiped (context) with a notebook to remember (memory), taught about your world (RAG) and the map of how it connects (knowledge graphs), able to reach your real tools through one standard plug (MCP).

That's not trivia. That's a **working understanding of how modern AI actually operates** — the thing that, a week ago, felt hopelessly out of reach. You can now follow an AI conversation, ask a sharp question, and smell nonsense when someone's overselling. The next lesson does two things: recaps this one final time in single lines, and shows you the doors out.

knowledgeCheck.stem: The course kept coming back to one image for what AI fundamentally is. Which was it?

options.text: A brilliant, well-read new hire — fast and capable, but a confident guesser who knows nothing about your specific business until you show it
An all-knowing oracle that always gives the correct, verified answer
A search engine that looks every answer up on the live internet
A calculator that only works with numbers and exact facts

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The spine of the whole course is AI as a capable new hire: quick and useful, but prone to confident guessing and clueless about your specifics until briefed — so you manage it, not blindly trust it. It's deliberately *not* an oracle (b): the guarantee of correctness is exactly what it lacks. It isn't a live-search engine (c) — it predicts likely words rather than fetching results. And it's the opposite of a numbers-only calculator (d); its strength is language.

knowledgeCheck.stem: You learned that context is "the desk" and memory is "the notebook beside it." What's the difference the two pictures capture?

options.text: Context is the short-term working space wiped clean at the end of each chat; memory is what gets saved so it survives into the next conversation
They're two names for exactly the same thing — the AI's permanent store of everything it has ever seen
Context is where your documents are permanently kept; memory is a setting that makes replies more creative
Context is the internet the AI searches; memory is the model's training data

knowledgeCheck.explanation: The desk (context) is the AI's here-and-now working space, cleared at the end of each chat; the notebook (memory) is the handful of useful things written down so they carry over next time. They're not the same thing (b) — that's the whole reason both pictures exist. Neither is where documents live permanently (c) — that's RAG and knowledge bases — and memory certainly isn't a creativity setting; that's the temperature knob. And neither one is the internet or the training data (d).

lessons.title: You're not an expert — you're literate. Pick your door.
lessons.objective: Recap all eleven ideas one final time in single lines, understand that completion here means literacy rather than expertise, and choose the right next door — AI Business Foundations, the practitioner lane, or Generative AI Leader — with confidence.
lessons.body:
## What "finishing" this course actually means

Let's be honest about what you've done, because the honesty is the point. You are **not** an AI expert now, and this course never promised to make you one. What you are is **AI-literate** — and that's a real, valuable thing, not a consolation prize.

Literacy means you can **define the words in your own language** and **hold your own in any AI conversation.** When someone at work says "we should put this behind RAG" or "what's the context window on that model," you no longer nod along pretending. You know what they mean. You can ask a sharp follow-up. You can tell when someone's overselling. That — being able to follow the conversation and choose your next step with confidence — **is** the hard part. The confusing wall you started at is behind you. Everything from here is just building on words you already own.

## The whole course, in eleven single lines

One last pass — the entire vocabulary, one line each, so you can hear how much you now hold:

1. **What AI is** — a well-read new hire that predicts likely next words: brilliant, tireless, a confident guesser.
2. **Agent** — that new hire given a job, the knowledge for it, and tools to actually do the work.
3. **Text, images, video** — the same trick pointed at different materials: words, pictures, footage.
4. **So many models** — different brands of a similar tool, each with its own strengths, like cars or phones.
5. **Which model** — pick by the job, and stay free to switch; there is no single "best."
6. **Open vs closed** — rent versus own: a service someone runs for you, or something you run yourself.
7. **The knobs** — mostly leave them alone; temperature is the one to know (careful vs creative).
8. **Context** — the desk the AI works at: bigger holds more, and it's wiped at the end of each chat.
9. **Memory** — the notebook beside the desk, where useful things are saved for next time.
10. **RAG** — teaching the AI your stuff by giving it your documents to look things up in.
11. **Knowledge graph** — the map of how your things connect, not just a pile of pages. And **MCP** — USB-C for AI tools: one standard plug so your AI can reach your real apps.

If those lines feel obvious now, that's the whole achievement. They were a foreign language a short while ago.

## Now pick your door

Here's the honest part, with no pressure and nothing to sell. Literacy is a **starting point**, and there are three sensible directions from here. Pick the one that fits what *you* actually want.

**Door one — you want to USE this in your business.** If you're an owner, a team lead, or a professional who just wants AI working *for* you, the next step is **AI Business Foundations**. It's the same plain-English style you just came through — no jargon, no exam — but hands-on, and you finish by **shipping one real automation** in your own business. Literacy in, a working result out. This is the door for most people who took this course to get something done.

**Door two — you want to go PRO and get certified.** If this lit a fire and you want to go deep — to build and design AI systems properly — there's a whole **practitioner lane** waiting, taking you from developer up to architect. It's more technical than what you've done, and that's the point: it's for people who want the real craft and a credential to prove it. You now have exactly the vocabulary you need to start it without drowning.

**Door three — you'd like a first credential, gently.** If you're business-minded and would like a **recognised certificate** to show for your understanding — but you don't want to go deep-technical — Google's **Generative AI Leader** is a friendly first exam. It's aimed at exactly the literacy you now have, dressed up for a business audience, and the academy can prep you for it. It's the natural bridge for the operator who wants a badge, not a codebase.

## That's it — you did the hard part

Whichever door you pick, walk through it knowing this: the confusing part is behind you. You came in not knowing the words, and you're leaving able to use the thing. **You're not an expert — you're literate. That's the hard part done. Pick your door.**

## The Automatos peek

Here's the quiet reward for finishing: every single idea in this course is something you can now go and *see* for free in Automatos. Talk to an agent and watch it do work rather than just chat. Upload a document and watch it answer with citations from your own file — that's RAG, live. Connect a tool and see the standard-plug idea in action. You arrived not knowing the words; you're leaving able to use the thing. Whichever door you pick, you'll recognise exactly what you're looking at — because you already own the vocabulary for all of it.

knowledgeCheck.stem: This module is honest that "completing" AI Explained makes you literate, not an expert. What does being AI-literate actually mean here?

options.text: You can define the core words in your own language, follow any AI conversation, and choose a sensible next step with confidence
You can now build and deploy production AI systems and write the code behind them
You've memorised technical definitions you can recite word-for-word on demand
You no longer need to learn anything further about AI — this is the finish line

knowledgeCheck.explanation: Literacy means you own the vocabulary in plain terms, can hold your own in the conversation, and can pick your next door — the honest, valuable outcome this course promises. It explicitly does *not* make you a builder of production systems (b): that's the practitioner lane, a further step. It isn't rote memorisation of jargon (c) — the whole method was plain-language understanding, not recital. And it's a starting point, not a finish line (d); the module's entire back half is about where to go next.

knowledgeCheck.stem: A shop owner finished this course and wants AI genuinely working in her business — but has no interest in coding or exams. Which door fits her best?

options.text: AI Business Foundations — plain-English and hands-on, where she finishes by shipping one real automation in her own business
The practitioner lane, going from developer to architect, because that's the only way to use AI seriously
Nothing — literacy is the end of the road, and there's no next step for a non-technical owner
She must pass Generative AI Leader before she's allowed to use AI in her business at all

knowledgeCheck.explanation: AI Business Foundations is built for exactly her — an owner who wants a working result, taught in the same plain style, ending in one real shipped automation, with no exam. The practitioner lane (b) is the deep-technical, get-certified path — overkill and mismatched for someone with no interest in coding. Literacy isn't the end of the road (c) — this module lays out three onward doors. And Generative AI Leader is an optional friendly credential, never a gate she must clear before using AI (d).

questions.stem: In one plain line, which of these best captures what an agent is — as this course taught it?

options.text: A well-read new hire given an actual job, the knowledge it needs, and tools it's allowed to use — so it does work, not just talk
Exactly the same thing as a chatbot: a program you type questions to and read answers from
A physical robot that carries out tasks in the real world
The training data an AI model learned from

questions.explanation: An agent is the capable new hire with a real job — a role, the knowledge for it, and tools — so it can *do* the work rather than only describe it. That's more than a chatbot, which just talks back (b): the job, knowledge, and tools are what make it an agent. It isn't a physical robot (c) — "agent" here is software with a purpose. And it isn't training data (d); that's what a model learned from, a different idea entirely.

questions.stem: Module 04 made a deliberate point of refusing to name a single "best" model. What was the honest takeaway?

options.text: Pick the model by the job in front of you, and stay free to switch — locking yourself to one forever is the real mistake
One model is clearly best for everything, and everyone should use only that one
All models are identical, so it never matters which you use for anything
You should always use the newest model released, whatever the task

questions.explanation: The honest answer is "it depends": choose for the job and keep the freedom to swap, since committing to one forever is the only genuine error. Crowning a single best-for-everything model (b) is exactly the claim the module refuses — strengths differ by task. Models aren't identical (c) — they have real different personalities and strengths — so the choice does matter. And "always newest" (d) is just another rigid rule; the point is to match the tool to the task, not chase releases.

questions.stem: You learned "open vs closed" through one everyday comparison. Which was it?

options.text: Rent versus own — a closed model you use as a service someone else runs; an open model you can take and run yourself
New versus second-hand — closed models are newer and open ones are older
Free versus paid — open always costs nothing and closed always costs money
Fast versus slow — closed models are always faster than open ones

questions.explanation: Rent versus own is the picture: closed means accessing a model as a hosted service, open means being able to run it yourself — different trade-offs, neither simply "better." It isn't about new versus old (b): age isn't what open/closed describes. It isn't strictly free versus paid (c) — running an open model yourself has its own costs, and the distinction is about control, not price. And it says nothing about speed (d).

questions.stem: Someone asks you to explain, in beginner terms, the difference between context and RAG. Which answer is right?

options.text: Context is the AI's short-term desk for the current chat, wiped when it ends; RAG is giving it your documents so it can look things up when it answers
They're the same thing under two names — both mean the AI's permanent memory
Context means giving it your documents, and RAG means the desk it works at during a chat
Both are settings you turn up to make the AI more creative in its replies

questions.explanation: Context is the here-and-now working desk that's cleared at the end of a chat; RAG is teaching the AI your world by giving it documents to search when answering — two different ideas. They're not the same thing (b), and neither is "permanent memory" (that's the notebook, memory). Option (c) simply swaps the two definitions — it has them backwards. And neither is a creativity dial (d); that's temperature, one of the knobs.

questions.stem: A colleague says a knowledge graph is "just a folder of all your documents in one place." Using what this course taught, what's the accurate correction?

options.text: It's more than a pile of documents — it's the map of how your things connect (which customer belongs to which account, which project links to which person), so the AI can follow the relationships
That's exactly right — a knowledge graph is simply a storage folder for files
A knowledge graph is the AI's short-term working desk during a single conversation
A knowledge graph is the standard plug that connects an AI to outside tools

questions.explanation: A knowledge graph is the map of connections between your things — the relationships, not just the pages — which is what lets the AI follow how one thing links to another. A plain folder of files (b) is closer to a document store or knowledge base; the graph adds the links between them. The short-term working desk (c) is context, a different idea. And the standard plug for tools (d) is MCP — also a different module. Each of those is a real concept you learned, just not this one.

questions.stem: A developer friend finished AI Explained and now wants to properly build and design AI systems, with a credential to prove it. Which door should you point them to?

options.text: The practitioner lane — the deeper, more technical path that takes you from developer to architect and certifies you
AI Business Foundations, because it's the only next step after AI Explained for anyone
Straight back to the start of AI Explained, since there's nowhere more technical to go
Nowhere — once you're AI-literate there are no further tracks to take

questions.explanation: The practitioner lane is exactly for this: someone who wants the real craft of building and designing AI systems, with a certification, progressing from developer to architect. AI Business Foundations (b) is the operator door — plain and hands-on for using AI in a business, not the deep-technical build path. Repeating AI Explained (c) makes no sense for someone ready to go deeper. And it's false that literacy is the end (d) — this module deliberately lays out onward doors, this being the technical one.

questions.stem: Which is the fair, honest description of what you've achieved by finishing AI Explained?

options.text: You're AI-literate — you can define the core ideas plainly, follow the conversation, and choose your next step; the confusing part is behind you
You're now a qualified AI expert able to architect production systems
You've learned a few buzzwords but can't really use any of them
You must still take an exam here before your understanding counts for anything

questions.explanation: The honest outcome is literacy: you own the vocabulary in plain terms and can pick your door — genuinely valuable, and the hard part done. It doesn't make you a production-system architect (b) — that's a further, deeper lane. It's more than a handful of hollow buzzwords (c): the course built a connected mental model you can actually use. And there's no exam gate here (d) — AI Explained is a no-exam skills track; your understanding is the point, not a certificate.

questions.stem: Where does Google's Generative AI Leader fit among the three doors this module names?

options.text: As a gentle first recognised credential for a business-minded person who wants a certificate without going deep-technical
As the hardest, most technical certification, meant for senior engineers only
As a mandatory exam everyone must pass before leaving AI Explained
As a replacement for AI Business Foundations that teaches the same hands-on automation

questions.explanation: Generative AI Leader is positioned as the friendly first credential — aimed at business-minded literacy, a badge to show for it, without deep technical depth. It's the opposite of an engineers-only, hardest certification (b) — that framing belongs to the practitioner lane. It's not mandatory or a gate on this course (c) — it's an optional onward door. And it isn't a stand-in for AI Business Foundations (d): that operator track is hands-on and ends in a shipped automation, whereas Generative AI Leader is an external exam you prep for.

resources.title: Automatos AI — About (what the platform is, in its own words)
resources.annotation: The platform's own plain-language tour — and a neat mirror of everything you just learned. Its glossary defines, in one line each, the very ideas this course taught: an agent is a worker with a model and tools (not a chatbot), knowledge powers everything via RAG, a knowledge graph is an auto-extracted map of how your entities connect, and memory is short-term (session) plus long-term (cross-conversation). Read it to see your new vocabulary reflected back in a real product, and as the grounding for this module's peek.

resources.title: Automatos Academy — Roadmap (the two lanes and where each door leads)
resources.annotation: The map behind this module's hand-off. It lays out the academy honestly: the Operator lane ("run your business with AI") starting at AI Business Foundations, the Practitioner lane ("get certified") going developer → architect, and Google's Generative AI Leader as the natural credential bridge for the business-minded. This is the source for the three doors — which is which, and who each is for — with no selling attached.

resources.title: Google — Generative AI Leader exam guide
resources.annotation: The friendly first credential named in door three. It's a vendor-light, business-toned exam aimed at exactly the literacy you now have — understanding what generative AI is and how it behaves, without deep-technical depth. Skim the guide to see whether a recognised certificate is a next step you'd enjoy; the academy can prep you for it. Named here as an optional onward door, never a gate on this course.

videos.title: M12 · You get it now — the whole vocabulary in your own words, in about 6 minutes
M12 · Send-off Deep Dive — literacy not expertise, and the two doors
